metadata.working_title: Effortless Landmark Detection (ELD): neural-network-guided thin-plate splines for spatial landmark discovery and tissue registration across modalities

abstract_sketch.problem: Spatial landmarks underpin comparison of histological heterogeneity across samples, tracking regions of interest in microscopy, and aligning tissue sections to a common coordinate framework, yet manual annotation remains a bottleneck for spatial omics.
abstract_sketch.gap: Existing unsupervised landmark detectors require very large training sets, struggle with the non-linear deformations characteristic of tissue sections, are poorly suited to z-stack alignment, and rarely cope with cross-modality data.
abstract_sketch.approach: We introduce ELD (Effortless Landmark Detection), which couples an hourglass-style landmark-detection network to a thin-plate spline (TPS) registration module and trains it end-to-end using a multi-scale structural-similarity (MS-SSIM) loss, landmark drop-out (p = 10%), and data augmentation including elastic deformation.
abstract_sketch.key_results: ELD achieves superior mean consistency and backward error on MAFL and AFLW face landmark benchmarks, matches or exceeds manual annotation for Eggplant-based Visium registration (three target genes: Nrgn, Apoe, Omp), outperforms STAlign on ISS data in two replicates using a k-nearest-neighbour region classifier, beats eight competing 3D-registration models on an Accumulated Target-Registration-Error (ATRE) benchmark across a 260-section mouse prostate dataset, and produces modality-agnostic landmarks across Visium-H&E and Visium-MSI pairs in developing heart and striatum tissue.
abstract_sketch.impact: By removing the generative network while keeping an unsupervised landmark detector, ELD turns small-sample spatial omics registration from a manual chore into an automatic, modality-agnostic pipeline that integrates cleanly with existing tools such as Eggplant, STAlign and CODA.

sections.title: Introduction
content_bullets: Spatial landmarks underpin histological comparison, ROI tracking in microscopy, and registration to a common coordinate framework
Manual landmark annotation is a bottleneck for spatial-omics scalability; automation would unlock larger experiments
Supervised landmark detection exists but is limited by labelled-data scarcity; unsupervised approaches combining a detector network with a generative model have shown promise

Three outstanding challenges: limited spatial-omics datasets (fewer than ten samples versus ~100,000 typical for deep learning), non-linear elastic-plus-rigid tissue deformations, and concurrent handling of histology, transcriptomics, and mass-spectrometry imaging modalities

Building on Sanchez et al., we introduce ELD which drops the generative head and couples the detector to a TPS registrar, yielding superior stability and efficiency across single-modality, 3D, and multimodal tasks

subsections.title: Benchmarking ELD against existing landmark-detection methods
content_bullets: Architectural justification: small-data generalisation achieved by removing the generative network, using dropout and lighter models; TPS replaces deep registration for elasticity with fewer unlearnable parameters
Workflow (Fig. 1a): detect or manually set landmarks on slices A and B, register B to A via TPS/Homography, then project to a common coordinate framework
Error metrics used: consistency, forward and backward errors (Sanchez et al. definitions), trained on CelebA, evaluated on MAFL and AFLW face-landmark datasets
ELD exhibits superior mean consistency error (Fig. 1b) with competing methods penalised by large outlier mis-localisations (Fig. 1c)
ELD yields significantly lower backward error (Fig. 1d) but marginally worse forward error (Fig. 1e), trading generalisation for landmark stability

Runtime: convergence time measured as a function of genes/channels (fixed 10 landmarks, Fig. 1f) and as a function of landmarks (fixed 3 channels, Fig. 1g); convergence criterion stringent yet empirically faster in real-world runs

subsections.title: Performance evaluation on single-modality data
content_bullets: Benchmark against Eggplant using manual annotation on 12 mouse olfactory-bulb Visium samples; targets Nrgn, Apoe, Omp with distinct expression patterns

ELD-derived landmarks match or exceed manual-annotation correlation (Fig. 2a–b); results hold when trained on histology, on 3 genes, or on 100 genes (PCA for visualisation, all genes used during training); 14 landmarks used throughout
ISS compatibility demonstrated on three mouse-brain coronal sections from Salas et al., using RGB images of ISS clustering as inputs and TPS for registration
Evaluation via k-nearest-neighbour anatomical-region classifier fit on the reference; ELD outperforms STAlign in both replicates (Fig. 2d)
Illustrative ELD-predicted landmarks on ISS-cluster RGB image shown in Fig. 2e

subsections.title: 3D modelling via anchor points
content_bullets: Modification for z-stack: landmarks become anchor points by constraining area change between TPS and rigid mappings (Fig. 3a,c)
Training loop: select a reference slice, make a noisy counterpart, detect landmarks on all three, map noisy source to reference, compare area change between TPS and rigid maps, iterate
Dataset: mouse prostate z-stack of 260 slices from Kartasalo et al. with dual-annotator landmarks; 20 landmarks used for alignment
Metrics: Target Registration Error (TRE) and Accumulated TRE (ATRE), both normalised to manual-landmark registration (score = 1); RMSE excluded because image preprocessing differed across methods

ELD TRE is comparable to competitors but ATRE is significantly lower, indicating consistent alignment across the full volume; compared against eight models (seven from Kartasalo et al. and CODA) (Fig. 3d)
Absolute error between manual and ELD alignment across four sections shown in Fig. 3e

subsections.title: Performance evaluation on multimodal data
content_bullets: Cross-modality training: two landmark detectors, one per modality; register sample A to sample B, assess similarity in detector latent space (Fig. 4a)
Human Developing Heart dataset (four samples): histology for two samples, Visium-derived images from MYH6, ELN, MYH7 for the others; 16 landmarks used; Fig. 4b
Eggplant correlation using ELD landmarks comparable to manual annotation (Fig. 4c)
MSI + Visium alignment on three mouse-striatum samples from Vicari et al.; PCA embeddings of each modality yield qualitatively consistent landmarks marking biologically relevant features (Fig. 4d)

sections.title: Methods
content_bullets: Hardware: NVIDIA A100-SXM 81GB GPU and 12 AMD EPYC 7742 64-core CPUs for training
Loss: MS-SSIM computed over multiple resolutions via the PIQA PyTorch package with default parameters and window size 5
Landmark drop-out with p = 10% escapes local minima where landmarks collapse onto similar positions
Cropping: mask pixels where all channels fall below threshold 0.1 and crop both images to the union mask to remove black-border augmentation artefacts
Registration: thin-plate spline minimising bending energy between n source and target points; weights solved analytically
Landmark detector: hourglass network with ~6 million parameters, identical to Sanchez et al.
Image preprocessing: 128x128 images via cv2.resize with INTER_AREA interpolation; orientation must be consistent (no flipping)
Data augmentation: random rotation in [-15, 15] degrees with compensating scaling; elastic deformation via the elasticdeform package with experiment-dependent control-point grid and sigma

Visium preprocessing: remove spots with fewer than 200 detected genes and genes present in fewer than three spots; normalise with Scanpy; log1p transform; for 3-gene experiments use Eggplant's gene list; for larger sets perform Leiden clustering on PCA-neighbour graphs, rank genes via Scanpy rank_genes_groups with t-test, then intersect top-n per sample across all samples

sections.title: Discussion
content_bullets: ELD solves small-sample and non-linear-deformation limitations by removing the generative network while retaining an unsupervised landmark detector
Versatility demonstrated across single-modality Visium and ISS, 3D prostate z-stacks, and multimodal H&E/Visium and MSI/Visium pairings

Landmark detection is the primary objective; registration via TPS is an added convenience. Integrating ELD's landmarks as ground truth during training of STAlign, CODA or similar may unlock further gains, analogous to Eggplant integration
Limitations include the requirement for consistent orientation, reliance on 128x128 inputs, and the need for per-modality detectors in cross-modality settings
Future directions: integrate with higher-capacity registration models; extend beyond bulk tissue to subcellular landmarks; evaluate on additional modalities

table_plan.data_source: experimental_log.md Methods: drop-out 10%, 128x128 input, rotation [-15,15], threshold 0.1, MS-SSIM window 5, ~6M-parameter hourglass, Visium filters (>=200 genes/spot, genes in >=3 spots)

macro_queries: spatial landmark detection deep learning histology
spatial omics transcriptomics common coordinate framework
micro_queries: in situ sequencing ISS spatial transcriptomics
multi-scale structural similarity MS-SSIM image quality
elastic deformation data augmentation medical imaging
must_cite: Sanchez et al. unsupervised landmark detector (hourglass architecture, basis for ELD)
Eggplant Visium registration tool (Mollbrink/Bergenstrahle style) for olfactory-bulb benchmark
STAlign spatial transcriptomics alignment method (ISS benchmark)
Salas mouse brain ISS dataset source
Vicari mouse striatum MSI + Visium multimodal dataset
Wang et al. MS-SSIM image quality metric
Keller et al. thin-plate spline registration
MSI mass spectrometry imaging technology reference
PIQA PyTorch image quality assessment package
